title: Cat Shaking Head Excessively: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat shaking their head? Discover common causes like ear mites or infections, and learn why this behavior usually requires a vet visit.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive head shaking in cats is a sign of ear pain or irritation that should be evaluated by a vet within 24–48 hours. Immediate care is needed if your cat also shows a head tilt or loss of balance.

What does it mean if my cat is shaking its head excessively?

When a cat shakes their head repeatedly, it is usually a sign of irritation, itchiness, or pain within the ear canal or on the ear flap. Common causes for this behavior include:

  • Ear mites or similar parasites
  • Bacterial or yeast infections
  • Inflammatory polyps
  • Foreign bodies, such as grass seeds, lodged in the ear canal

Is my cat’s head shaking a veterinary emergency?

While head shaking is typically not a life-threatening emergency, it indicates significant discomfort. You should take the following steps:

  • Schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours to prevent complications like ear hematomas or middle-ear infections.
  • Monitor for a persistent head tilt or loss of balance, which requires more prompt veterinary attention.
  • Avoid delay to ensure the underlying cause does not progress to a more serious condition.

How does taking a photo of my cat's ear help with the triage process?

A clear, well-lit photo of the inside of your cat's ear is a valuable tool for triage professionals. A quality image allows them to:

  • Identify the specific type of discharge, such as dark, crumbly debris from mites or moist, yellow discharge from infection.
  • Assess the degree of redness and swelling inside the ear.
  • Determine the urgency of the situation and how quickly your pet needs to be seen by a veterinarian.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, excessive head shaking can be a sign of otitis media or externa, often accompanied by rubbing or scratching the affected ear, and tilting or rotating the head toward the affected side. Clinical signs may include unilateral chronic otic discharge (ceruminous, purulent, mucoid, or hemorrhagic) and necrotic odor. Aural hematomas can result from head shaking. If the middle or inner ear is involved, neurologic signs such as deafness, vestibular signs (head tilt, ataxia, nystagmus), facial nerve palsy or paralysis (facial drooping, salivation, and dropping food from the lips), Horner syndrome (eyelid droop, pinpoint pupil, and inward rolling of the globe), and occasional protrusion of the third eyelid may be present. Nasopharyngeal polyps (more common in younger cats) and ear canal tumors (more common in middle-aged to older cats) should also be considered, as these can cause similar signs, including head shaking.

Chapter: Neurology, Behavioral, Respiratory, Gastroenterology, Ophthalmology, Otorhinolaryngology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1549)

faqs.question: Can I use human ear drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter drops without a vet's approval, as some ingredients can be toxic to cats or damaging if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: Are ear mites contagious to my other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear mites are highly contagious between cats and dogs. If one pet is diagnosed, all pets in the household usually require treatment.

faqs.question: How can I tell if my cat has an ear infection?
faqs.answer: Common signs include a foul odor from the ear, redness of the ear flap, discharge (black, yellow, or brown), and sensitivity when the head is touched.
